design_principles: Domain events are intra-component signals — they do not drive cross-context communication
The event publisher port belongs to the application layer — use cases own the decision of when to drain and publish events
The publisher implementation belongs to the shell layer — it is the only layer permitted to import from multiple bounded contexts
Events carry immutable data snapshots — the event payload is not modified after emission
Publishing must never interrupt the main pipeline — a publisher implementation failure must be silently swallowed
No domain logic lives in publisher implementations — they are pure observability transports
A single shared implementation instance serves all bounded contexts — one LoggingEventPublisher is wired to all use cases in the composition root
The drain-and-publish pattern is the only emission mechanism — aggregates accumulate events internally; use cases drain them at the boundary after domain operations complete

architectural_decision.description: A minimal in-process synchronous publisher. Aggregates raise events into an internal list. Use cases drain that list via collect_events() and pass it to IEventPublisher.publish() immediately after the domain operation returns. The publisher writes each event to a structured file log.
rationale: The RAG pipeline is inherently synchronous — evidence retrieval must complete before response generation. An async event bus would introduce unnecessary complexity with no benefit.

Domain events in this system are observability signals, not behavioral triggers. GapDetected, for example, surfaces as a DTO field (TurnIntelligenceResultDTO.clarification_needed) — not as a cross-context event. The GapDetected domain event is logged for audit, not routed to a handler.
Cross-context communication is defined exclusively through direct port calls (COMM-1, COMM-2). Events do not drive any inter-context coordination.
The prototype scope does not require event replay, durable messaging, or fan-out delivery. These are premature for the current stage.
No external broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, Redis Streams) is needed or appropriate. Adding one would be over-engineering.

rejected_alternatives.alternative: Full in-process event bus with subscribe/dispatch pattern
rejected_alternatives.reason: No handlers trigger business logic in response to domain events. Subscribe/dispatch machinery adds complexity without a consumer that needs it.
No async workflow requirements. The system is a synchronous API server with a single-process architecture. A broker would require infrastructure the prototype does not need.
Context communication is defined as synchronous direct interface calls. Routing events across contexts would violate the communication model defined in context_communication.json.

interface.contract: Receives the full list of domain events drained from one aggregate in a single use-case boundary crossing. Must not raise — silently handle all internal failures.
Receives the full list of domain events drained from DecisionContext after ProcessTurnIntelligenceUseCase completes the domain operations for the turn.

consumed_events.note: Emitted on every turn that adds at least one PreferenceSignal

Emitted only when gap evaluation finds context insufficient for retrieval intent. The behavioral consequence (clarification_needed=true) is delivered via TurnIntelligenceResultDTO — not via the event.
Emitted when DecisionContext transitions to ready state for the first time. Signals that retrieval quality is sufficient for grounded response generation.

event_publisher_implementations.name: LoggingEventPublisher
behavior: Accepts the drained event list from any use case — BC-1 or BC-2
Iterates over each event and serialises it with dataclasses.asdict()
Writes a structured INFO-level log line per event via Python logging
Never raises — all exceptions are swallowed to protect the main pipeline
Configures a FileHandler once on construction — idempotent (no duplicate handlers if already registered)
Directory for the log file is created if it does not exist

wiring.instantiation: Single instance created in composition root

domain_event_registry.name: SessionStarted
domain_event_registry.observability_purpose: Audit trail for session creation. Enables counting active sessions, measuring session frequency per user, and detecting initialization failures by absence.

domain_event_registry.name: TurnProcessed
domain_event_registry.observability_purpose: Audit trail for every completed turn. Enables intent distribution analysis, conversation length tracking, and per-session activity timelines.

domain_event_registry.name: SessionClosed
domain_event_registry.observability_purpose: Audit trail for session closure. Enables session duration calculation and detection of sessions that are opened but never explicitly closed.

domain_event_registry.name: ContextUpdated
domain_event_registry.observability_purpose: Tracks preference signal accumulation across turns. Enables analysis of how many turns it takes for users to reach context readiness and which signal types are most frequently captured.

domain_event_registry.name: GapDetected
domain_event_registry.emitted_by: DecisionContext.record_gap_evaluation() — conditional on gaps being non-empty
domain_event_registry.observability_purpose: Audit trail for clarification requests. Enables tracking how often context gaps block retrieval, which gap types recur most frequently, and whether clarification loops converge.
domain_event_registry.behavioral_note: The clarification_needed=true behavioral consequence is delivered synchronously via TurnIntelligenceResultDTO — not triggered by this event. The event is purely for observability.

domain_event_registry.name: ContextReady
domain_event_registry.observability_purpose: Milestone event marking the first turn where the context reached retrieval readiness. Enables measuring how many turns were required to build sufficient context for grounded retrieval.

event_flow.description: Aggregates accumulate domain events in an internal list during domain operations. At the use-case boundary, after the domain method returns, the use case drains the list by calling collect_events() and passes the full batch to IEventPublisher.publish(). The publisher processes all events in the batch synchronously before returning.
lifecycle_steps: 1. ConversationSession or DecisionContext raises a domain event internally via self._events.append(event)
2. The domain operation (e.g. complete_turn()) returns to the use case
3. The use case calls aggregate.collect_events() — drains and clears the internal list
5. LoggingEventPublisher iterates the batch and writes one log line per event to data/domain_events.log
6. Control returns to the use case — the event lifecycle is complete
event_flow.guarantee: At-most-once delivery. Events are not persisted before publishing — if the process crashes between drain and publish, those events are lost. Acceptable for an observability-only log at prototype scope.
event_flow.batch_semantics: Events are published as a batch per aggregate drain, not individually. This ensures that all events from a single domain operation are written in order with no interleaving from other operations.

in_scope: Domain event emission from ConversationSession and DecisionContext aggregates
IEventPublisher port in BC-1 and BC-2 application layers
LoggingEventPublisher shell implementation writing to data/domain_events.log
Constructor injection of the publisher into all four event-emitting use cases
out_of_scope: Cross-context event routing — inter-context communication uses direct port calls (COMM-1, COMM-2)
Event-driven handlers that trigger business logic — no such handlers exist in this system
Event replay or event sourcing — not required at prototype scope
Async event delivery — the synchronous RAG pipeline has no async requirements
External message brokers — Kafka, RabbitMQ, Redis Streams are out of scope
Subscribe/dispatch infrastructure — there are no subscribers; the port method is publish(), not subscribe()
